_doc: Skill quality-gate scenarios for the jobs_skill.py system prompt (services/chat-service/app/services/jobs_skill.py) — each scenario targets a specific stated rule an agent could plausibly violate; context is always null because jobs_* is account-level/cross-service, not book-scoped, so enabled_skills=["jobs"] is what forces the pin.

scenarios.ground_truth: Per jobs_skill.py's 'Controlling jobs' section: there is NO `jobs_resume` or `jobs_retry` tool — resuming a paused job is a DOMAIN-specific action. The agent must NOT invent/call a `jobs_resume` tool (it does not exist and would be a hallucinated call), and must NOT flatly tell the user resuming is impossible. Correct behavior: recognize this is a paused translation job and point the user to the domain's OWN control tool — `translation_job_control(action="resume")` — rather than the generic `jobs_*` system. Bonus correctness: note that resuming (unlike pausing) may re-spend money and typically needs its own confirm_action, so the agent shouldn't just fire it silently. Reply text alone (no tool call) recommending the right tool/path is acceptable; a call to a nonexistent `jobs_resume`/`jobs_retry` tool is a hard failure.
turns: Can you resume my paused translation job? I think it stopped a while ago.

scenarios.ground_truth: Per jobs_skill.py's 'Controlling jobs' section: `jobs_cancel` and `jobs_pause` are NOT equally reversible — pause can be undone (resume lives on the domain's own control tool), but cancel is TERMINAL (a cancelled job cannot be un-cancelled, only restarted from scratch). The user said only 'stop' — ambiguous between the two. Correct behavior: the agent should ask the user to clarify whether they want a reversible pause or a permanent cancel BEFORE acting, since the two have very different consequences. It must not silently default to jobs_cancel (the destructive, non-reversible action) without warning or clarification. If the agent does infer cancel is intended, it must explicitly warn that cancel is terminal/can't be walked back before (or while) calling jobs_cancel — telling the user only after calling it is a rule violation ('Tell the user cancel can't be walked back BEFORE you call it, not after').
turns: Stop my job, it's job_id abc123 on the translation service.

scenarios.ground_truth: Per jobs_skill.py's 'Owner-scoped, always' section: every jobs_* tool is filtered to the caller's own jobs automatically (no user id is ever passed), and if a service+job_id doesn't exist OR belongs to someone else, BOTH cases return the identical `{"success": false, "error": "not found or not accessible"}` — a deliberate anti-oracle. The agent should call jobs_get (or jobs_list) with the given service+job_id, and if it comes back not found/not accessible, the reply must NOT claim to know which case it is — it must not say 'that job doesn't exist' nor 'that job belongs to someone else,' since the tool result is uninformative about which. Correct phrasing acknowledges the job isn't visible/accessible to the user's account without asserting a specific reason. A confident claim of exactly why (job never existed vs owned by another user) is a hard failure.
turns: Can you check on job xyz-999 on the extraction service? A friend told me the id but I want to see its status.

scenarios.ground_truth: Per jobs_skill.py's 'Controlling jobs' section: only multi-unit jobs (campaigns, multi-chapter runs) can be paused — a single-call job is cancel-only, and `control_caps` (returned by jobs_get) tells you which actions are CURRENTLY valid, so the agent should check control_caps before calling jobs_cancel/jobs_pause rather than guessing (calling an action not in control_caps is refused, not silently ignored). This is a single one-off cover-art generation job, i.e. NOT multi-unit. Correct behavior: the agent calls jobs_get(service, job_id) first to inspect control_caps rather than assuming jobs_pause will work, and if pause is not in control_caps, explains to the user that this job is cancel-only (single-unit jobs can't be paused) instead of calling jobs_pause blindly or claiming success on a pause that would be refused.
turns: Please pause my cover art generation job, service is media, job id is media-job-42. It's just the one image, not a batch.

scenarios.ground_truth: Per jobs_skill.py's 'A domain may have its OWN separate job view' section: translation exposes its OWN job tools (translation_job_status/translation_job_control) alongside the generic jobs_* system, and the two are DIFFERENT SHAPES over related but not identical data — an id obtained from jobs_list should not be fed into a domain-specific tool expecting jobs_list's shape, or vice versa. The user asks a mixed question: a cross-service 'what's running' plus a specific translation job id. Correct behavior: use jobs_list/jobs_summary (the generic system) to answer the 'what's running right now across everything' part, and if the user wants translation-specific detail/actions on the translation job id (e.g. richer status, resume/retry), use translation_job_status/translation_job_control for that piece rather than assuming jobs_get on that id returns the same shape as translation's own status tool, or vice versa. Conflating the two shapes (e.g. treating a translation_job_status field as if it came from jobs_get, or querying jobs_get and asserting translation-specific fields it doesn't return) is a failure.
turns: What's running right now across all my stuff? Also my translation job trans-777 — can you give me its full detail, like the translation-specific status?

scenarios.ground_truth: Per jobs_skill.py's 'Live progress is a different tool' section: jobs_list/jobs_get answer on-demand 'what's the status' questions, but LIVE auto-updating progress the user watches in the UI is a SEPARATE frontend tool, `ui_watch_job` — meant to be called right after starting a job, not as a substitute for querying jobs_* afterward. The user explicitly asks to 'watch' progress live/continuously. Correct behavior: the agent should mention/use `ui_watch_job` as the mechanism for live, auto-updating progress rather than proposing to repeatedly poll jobs_get/jobs_list in a loop, and should not conflate a one-shot jobs_get status check with genuine live progress-watching. Merely calling jobs_get once and calling that 'watching' (with no mention of ui_watch_job) is a partial failure; describing a manual polling loop as the solution is a failure.
turns: I just kicked off a big extraction job — can you watch its progress live for me so I don't have to keep asking?
